attackText: P3 must be struck out: it founders on the parties' own contract. Clause 3.1 of the Master Services Agreement (D03) imposes only a "reasonable endeavours" obligation toward a "target go-live date" and expressly states the timetable "is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure," and SOW-01 (D04) repeats verbatim that "The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date." Language of "reasonable endeavours," "target" and "planning estimate" is the antithesis of time being of the essence, and in any event the 1 October date was lawfully superseded by signed Change Order No. 3 (D07), which Meridian's own Programme Director Ms Vance (D16) and signatory Ms Nair concede moved go-live to 18 November 2024. On no admissible construction of Meridian's own documents can it sustain a plea that 1 October was a fixed date with time of the essence, and the allegation discloses no reasonable cause of action.
killshotQuote: The parties acknowledge that the implementation timetable is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure.
fixSuggestion: Abandon the "time of the essence" framing of P3 now, before trial; it is unwinnable on our own contract and signed change order, and pressing it will bleed credibility into the genuinely contestable heads (quantum, the 40% figure). Recast the timing case, if at all, as a reasonable-endeavours breach measured against the contractually revised 18 November date under Change Order No. 3 - but be candid that even that is weak given D09 shows our own director directed go-live over TechFlow's deferral advice. If timing is to be run, first obtain any pre-contract correspondence or board papers showing the parties treated 1 October as a hard commercial deadline, and serve a notice making time of the essence in proper form; absent such evidence, drop the plea and re-plead around it.

attackText: P4 should be struck out: it pleads an oral pre-contractual representation that the Platform would handle 10,000 transactions, yet identifies no maker, no date, no document and no witness who heard it, and clause 22.2 of the Master Services Agreement is a clear contractual estoppel — Meridian "acknowledges that it has not relied on, and shall have no remedy in respect of, any representation or statement not expressly set out in this Agreement (save in the case of fraud)." The 10,000-transaction figure appears nowhere in the executed contract, so absent a properly particularised and pleaded case in fraud — which is not advanced — Meridian is contractually barred from relying on it. A bare allegation of an unevidenced oral representation, met by an express non-reliance clause, discloses no reasonable cause of action and should be struck out or summarily dismissed.
killshotQuote: Each party acknowledges that it has not relied on, and shall have no remedy in respect of, any representation or statement not expressly set out in this Agreement (save in the case of fraud).
fixSuggestion: Right-size or abandon P4 as currently framed. As a negligent/innocent misrepresentation plea it is dead on the face of clause 22.2, so do not run it on that basis. Before trial, decide whether you can properly plead and particularise FRAUD (the only carve-out): you would need a named individual at TechFlow (e.g. Frost), the exact words used, the date and setting, and a witness or contemporaneous note evidencing both that the 10,000-transaction statement was made and that the maker knew it was false or was reckless. If you cannot evidence each of those elements to the heightened pleading standard for fraud, withdraw P4 rather than have it struck out with costs, and redirect the unavailability/performance grievance into the breach-of-contract and defect claims where it can stand on the documents.

attackText: Ms Vance, Change Order No. 3 — signed for Meridian by your own Head of Procurement, Priya Nair, on 2 September 2024 — revised the go-live date "from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024" and provided that "The revised date applies to the Platform as a whole"; do you accept that 18 November was therefore the contractually agreed go-live date, not 1 October? And on 24 October 2024, faced with TechFlow's written reservations, was it not you who wrote "Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team's reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian's side"? So when your pleaded case calls 18 November "roughly seven weeks late," you are in truth complaining that TechFlow delivered on the very date your company contractually fixed and that you personally insisted upon over TechFlow's advice — that is right, isn't it?
killshotQuote: Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side.
fixSuggestion: Abandon the "delivered ~7 weeks late" framing for the 18 November go-live — it is fatally contradicted by your own signed Change Order No. 3 (D07) and by Vance's and Nair's witness statements (D16, D18), and the D09 email makes a "TechFlow's delay" narrative untenable. Do not let Vance be cross-examined on this as pleaded. If any lateness case survives at all, recast it narrowly as slippage measured against the contractually revised 18 November baseline (e.g. specific defect-driven delay against post-Change-Order milestones), and obtain a programme/scheduling expert to isolate any delay genuinely attributable to TechFlow rather than to Meridian's own loyalty-module scope addition or its director's risk-on-our-side go-live decision; otherwise drop P5 and concentrate resources on quantum.

attackText: Ms Vance, your pleaded case is that Meridian never at any time requested any change to the agreed scope and that all delay was TechFlow's fault. Can I show you Change Order No. 3, which records "At the Customer's request" and names the requestor as "Helena Vance, Programme Director, Meridian Retail Group plc" — that is you, is it not? And before that, on your own email, you wrote that "the board wants a customer loyalty capability included in the platform before we go live", that you "appreciate this was not in the original SOW", and you asked TechFlow to "scope this as a change" — so it was Meridian, through you, that requested the out-of-scope loyalty module, and the delay you now blame on TechFlow was the direct consequence of your own change, was it not?
killshotQuote: the board wants a customer loyalty capability included in the platform before we go live ... I appreciate this was not in the original SOW ... Can you scope this as a change
fixSuggestion: Abandon P6 as pleaded — it is an absolute "no scope change ever requested" allegation that is flatly contradicted by your own signed Change Order No. 3 and Helena Vance's originating email, and running it will hand the defence a free credibility win that taints the rest of your case. Recast the delay claim, if at all, as a narrower plea that even allowing for the agreed loyalty-module variation TechFlow's resourcing caused delay beyond the revised 18 November date — but only if you can obtain independent project-management/delay-analysis evidence isolating TechFlow-attributable slippage from the scope change Meridian itself instigated. Do not let Vance be cross-examined on the current plea.

attackText: Ms Vance, your pleaded case is that Meridian repeatedly warned TechFlow the platform was not ready and pressed it to defer go-live, which TechFlow ignored. Yet in your own witness statement you say "Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025... I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024... I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow's advice." And in TechFlow's email of 24 October 2024 it was your side, not theirs, that wrote "Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned" over their clear recommendation to defer. So it was TechFlow that warned and urged deferral, and you who overruled them and proceeded — the precise opposite of what Meridian has pleaded, isn't it?
killshotQuote: Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025... I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024... I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow's advice.
fixSuggestion: Abandon P7 as pleaded — it is not weak, it is affirmatively false and contradicted by your own Programme Director's signed statement and the contemporaneous 24 October 2024 email; running it invites a credibility finding that will taint the whole claim. Do not try to recast it as "TechFlow should have refused to go live" — the 24 October email shows TechFlow expressly advised against it and Meridian accepted the risk, so that argument fails too. If any go-live theory survives, it must be re-pleaded narrowly around TechFlow's underlying readiness/defect failures (P8/P13 territory) decoupled from the deferral narrative; before doing so, obtain a frank privileged review of all October-November 2024 correspondence to confirm there is no contemporaneous document in which Meridian, not TechFlow, urged deferral — if none exists, the deferral plea cannot be saved.

attackText: Ms Vance, your pleaded case is that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours. But in your own witness statement you say you "could not say for how long in total" — so where does the 40% figure come from, if not from you? And you accept "some of the connectivity problems were to do with our own store broadband rather than the platform itself" — yet your own Platform defect log records that "the single largest outage (D-003) and the intermittent till freezes (D-006) were attributable to network connectivity provided by Meridian, not to the Platform." On those documents the platform-attributable downtime is modest, the biggest outage on 25 November was your own provider Northgate Telecom failing — so the 40% figure is one you cannot support and your own evidence contradicts, is that not right?
killshotQuote: I could not say for how long in total, and I understand some of the connectivity problems were to do with our own store broadband rather than the platform itself
fixSuggestion: Abandon the precise "over 40% of trading hours" figure — it is unsupported by any witness and is actively contradicted by your own defect log (D13) and Vance's statement (D16). Before trial, get a properly instructed independent IT/availability expert to quantify, from server and network logs, only the downtime genuinely attributable to Platform defects (excluding the Northgate Telecom WAN failure on 25 Nov and store-broadband outages), and re-plead a defensible, evidenced percentage. If the expert figure is materially below 40% (likely ~6%), recast P8 around the specific Platform-caused defects rather than a global availability claim, and concede the network-attributable outages to preserve credibility on the rest.

attackText: Ms Nair, your client's pleaded case is that Meridian never gave any sign-off or acceptance of the Platform "of any kind." But at paragraph of your own witness statement you say, in terms, that on 12 November 2024 you signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate. I am going to show you that certificate, document D08, which records that "Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted" — that is your signature on it, is it not, as Head of Procurement? So the pleaded allegation that no acceptance was ever given is, on your own evidence and your own signature, simply untrue.
killshotQuote: On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing. There were some minor snags listed, but no critical items were outstanding at that point.
fixSuggestion: Abandon the absolute "no sign-off ever" formulation immediately — it is flatly contradicted by your own signatory (Nair, D18) and the signed certificate (D08), and pressing it will cost you credibility on every other plea. Recast P10 narrowly and honestly: acceptance of Phase 1 was qualified and conditional ("subject to the resolution of the outstanding minor Severity 3 and 4 items"), and there was never any acceptance of the Platform as a whole or of later phases. Before trial, obtain (a) the contractual definition of "Acceptance" and whether Phase 1 acceptance is severable from whole-Platform acceptance, and (b) the closure/non-closure record for the Severity 3/4 snags, so you can argue the certificate's express conditions were never met rather than denying the signature exists.

attackText: P11 alleges TechFlow failed to provide adequate training, but it pleads a duty that does not exist. Clause-for-clause, Meridian's own Statement of Work (SOW-01) places end-user training squarely on Meridian and confines TechFlow's obligation to a single train-the-trainer session and written user guides. On the face of the contract Meridian itself drafted into the bundle, there is no pleaded breach to try: a failure to provide "adequate training" of store and head-office staff is a failure of Meridian's own contractual responsibility, not TechFlow's. Unless and until Meridian pleads that TechFlow failed to deliver the train-the-trainer session or the user guides specifically, P11 discloses no reasonable cause of action and should be struck out.
killshotQuote: Training of the Customer's store and head-office staff in the use of the Platform is the responsibility of the Customer. The Supplier shall provide a train-the-trainer session and written user guides only.
fixSuggestion: Abandon the broad "inadequate training" plea as currently framed — SOW-01 puts end-user training on Meridian and caps TechFlow's duty at a train-the-trainer session plus written guides, so the plea is dead on Meridian's own document. If there is any case at all, right-size it: re-plead narrowly that TechFlow failed to deliver the train-the-trainer session or the written user guides, or that what was delivered was non-conforming. Before doing so, get evidence that the train-the-trainer session was not held or that the guides were absent/defective (attendance records, calendar entries, the guides themselves, contemporaneous complaints). Without that, drop P11 rather than expose the rest of the pleading to a credibility hit.

attackText: Ms Nair, you tell the court Meridian's £1.8 million was "wasted expenditure" — but in your own statement you say you "withheld the final two milestone payments of £300,000 each — £600,000 in total — because of the problems we experienced after go-live." If the platform was truly worthless to you, why did you not also demand back the £1.8m you had already paid, instead of simply keeping the £600,000 in your pocket against value you say you never received? And this is the same Phase 1 you personally signed off on 12 November 2024 as having "passed user acceptance testing" with "no critical items outstanding" — so the expenditure on a Phase 1 you accepted as fit for purpose was not wasted at all, was it?
killshotQuote: We withheld the final two milestone payments of £300,000 each — £600,000 in total — because of the problems we experienced after go-live.
fixSuggestion: Do not run £1.8m as "wholly wasted" — it is internally inconsistent with retaining £600k and with Nair's signed Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate (D08), and TechFlow will set off the unpaid £600k milestones and invoke the clause 14 cap (charges actually paid). Right-size the plea before trial: recast it as the net diminution in value (price paid less value of the Phase 1 work actually accepted and used), expressly account for the £600k withheld so you are not seen to double-recover, and instruct a quantum expert to evidence what proportion of the £1.8m bought genuinely unusable deliverables. Also be ready to argue clause 14's enforceability under UCTA, because if the cap holds your recoverable ceiling is the charges paid in any event.

attackText: This head of loss should be struck out: it is barred on the face of the parties' own contract. Clause 14.1 of the Master Services Agreement provides in terms that "the Supplier shall not be liable to the Customer, whether in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise, for any loss of profit, loss of revenue, loss of anticipated savings, or any indirect or consequential loss" — which is precisely and exclusively the loss the Claimant pleads. No fraud is alleged, so the exclusion stands, and the entire GBP 4.2m claim falls away as a matter of construction without the court needing to hear a word of evidence. And even if it survived, it is a phantom figure: the Claimant's own quantum expert, Ms Greenhalgh FCA, opines that the loss "capable of being attributed to the Platform's defects is of the order of GBP 1,300,000, not GBP 4,200,000" — the Claimant has pleaded a number its own witness disowns by roughly threefold.
killshotQuote: In my opinion the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated... the incremental lost gross profit capable of being attributed to the Platform's defects is of the order of £1,300,000, not £4,200,000.
fixSuggestion: Do not run the GBP 4.2m loss-of-profit figure as pleaded — it is indefensible and invites a strike-out plus a credibility hit on every other head. First, you must plead and properly particularise a case that clause 14.1 is unenforceable (e.g. UCTA reasonableness) or that it does not on its true construction reach this loss; without that the claim is dead on the documents. Second, right-size the quantum to your own expert's GBP 1.3m before trial, and obtain a supplementary report from Greenhalgh that expressly carves out and quantifies the Lutterworth DC flood, the sector downturn and competitor discounting (per Okafor's statement and the Q4 internal email), so the residual figure survives the obvious causation attack rather than being demolished by your own director's admission that she "would be wary of attributing the whole shortfall to the platform".
